question: What are the main failure modes of memory in LLM agents?

heuristic_evaluation.question: What are the main failure modes of memory in LLM agents?

coverage.aspect: retrieval of stale or irrelevant memories
trade-offs between short-term and long-term memory

claim_lines: The main failure modes of memory in LLM agents are not just forgetting; they are failures in what gets written, what gets retrieved, and what gets trusted. Short-term memory helps with the current interaction, while long-term memory extends state across sessions, but it also introduces staleness, retrieval noise, and extra maintenance cost. https://openreview.net/forum?id=KPs1EgGKcT https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.08560 ([openreview.net](https://openreview.net/forum?id=KPs1EgGKcT))

- context pollution — irrelevant or stale memories can leak into the active context and distort decisions; OpenAI’s SDK explicitly warns that memory can become stale and should be treated as guidance, not truth. https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/sandbox/memory/ ([openai.github.io](https://openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/sandbox/memory/))

- retrieval of stale or irrelevant memories — similarity-based retrieval can surface outdated but semantically similar experiences, leading to misaligned replay and outdated answers. https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.16067 ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.16067))

- over-trusting previous agent outputs — once old runs are written back into memory, their errors can compound; the empirical study calls this error propagation. https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.16067 ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.16067))

- trade-offs between short-term and long-term memory — short-term memory preserves multi-turn state, while long-term memory spans sessions but requires tiering, update logic, and careful retrieval to avoid stale context. https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/add-memory https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.08560 ([docs.langchain.com](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/add-memory))

- evaluation methods for memory quality — use benchmarks that test accurate retrieval, test-time learning, long-range understanding, and conflict resolution; survey work also warns that many benchmarks are underscaled or metric-misaligned. https://huggingface.co/papers/2507.05257 https://openreview.net/forum?id=KPs1EgGKcT ([huggingface.co](https://huggingface.co/papers/2507.05257))
In one line: memory fails when it is noisy, stale, over-accepted, or poorly evaluated.
unsupported_claim_lines: In one line: memory fails when it is noisy, stale, over-accepted, or poorly evaluated.

coverage_audit.reason: Explicitly describes irrelevant or stale memories leaking into active context and distorting decisions.

coverage_audit.aspect: retrieval of stale or irrelevant memories
coverage_audit.reason: Directly addresses similarity-based retrieval surfacing outdated or semantically similar but irrelevant memories.
Explains compounding errors when prior agent outputs are written back into memory and trusted too much.

coverage_audit.aspect: trade-offs between short-term and long-term memory
coverage_audit.reason: States the key trade-off: short-term preserves current multi-turn state, long-term spans sessions but adds staleness and maintenance/retrieval complexity.
Mentions benchmarks and evaluation dimensions, but the guidance is somewhat high-level and not deeply operationalized.

citation_audit.claim: Context pollution: irrelevant or stale memories can leak into the active context and distort decisions.
citation_audit.reason: Framework docs plausibly support the notion that memory can become stale and should not be treated as ground truth.

citation_audit.claim: Retrieval of stale or irrelevant memories can surface outdated but semantically similar experiences, leading to misaligned replay and outdated answers.
citation_audit.reason: An empirical study of memory management is a credible source for retrieval/pathology claims.

citation_audit.claim: Once old runs are written back into memory, their errors can compound; this is error propagation.
citation_audit.reason: The cited empirical report is a plausible basis for compounding-error behavior.

citation_audit.claim: Short-term memory preserves multi-turn state, while long-term memory spans sessions but requires tiering, update logic, and careful retrieval to avoid stale context.
citation_audit.reason: LangGraph docs and an agent memory survey are relevant sources for architectural trade-offs.

citation_audit.claim: Use benchmarks that test accurate retrieval, test-time learning, long-range understanding, and conflict resolution; many benchmarks are underscaled or metric-misaligned.
citation_audit.reason: Relevant benchmark/survey sources, but the answer gives broad categories without showing that the specific cited paper covers each criterion in the same terms.

citation_audit.claim: In one line: memory fails when it is noisy, stale, over-accepted, or poorly evaluated.
citation_audit.reason: This is a summary statement not directly grounded by a specific citation.

source_audit.reason: ArXiv paper is directly relevant as an agent memory survey.

Implementation documentation is relevant to practical memory trade-offs.

Paper listing/summary page rather than the paper itself; less ideal as evidence for detailed evaluation guidance.

judge_evaluation.summary: Good coverage and structure with mostly relevant sources, but the evaluation guidance is somewhat shallow and one summary claim is not directly supported.
judge_evaluation.recommended_fix: Ground the evaluation section in a concrete benchmark or paper result, and remove or cite the unsupported one-line summary so every nontrivial claim is explicitly supported.
